video_title: The Investor Betting BILLIONS on AI
gist: Union Square Ventures GP Nick Grossman lays out USV's 'Rebel Alliance' thesis: AI is too big to be monopolized by two or three frontier labs, so durable non-lab companies will emerge at the application, middleware, model and energy layers, with value increasingly driven by multi-agent orchestration and multi-model routing. He and host Anthony Pompliano dig into the 'AI kill zone', agentic investing, data monetization, and the geopolitical and social trade-offs of the buildout.

themes.theme: Rebel Alliance thesis vs lab monopoly

insights.claim: USV's 'Rebel Alliance' thesis holds that AI is too large an opportunity to be dominated by two or three companies and is instead forming a massive ecosystem of consumer-facing agents (AI doctors, financial advisors) and infrastructure agents embedded under the hood of every product.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Frames USV's investing posture against the consensus bet that AI value collapses into a few frontier labs; look for durable non-lab winners at multiple layers.
insights.quote: the Rebel Alliance is our view that uh AI is too big an opportunity to be dominated by one or two or three companies and that it's actually forming into a massive ecosystem

insights.claim: Grossman concedes real network effects exist in AI model training that have compounded advantage to the big labs, but argues the countervailing forces are strong competition among frontier models, open-source models improving in lockstep, and data value accruing to the application layer rather than only the model layer.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Identifies where non-lab value can accrue; the bet that data advantage disperses across layers is the crux of whether app-layer startups are investable.
insights.quote: there's going to be an unassalable gravity into the big AI companies

insights.claim: Grossman rejects Peter Thiel's 'competition is for losers' monopoly framing as the end state for AI, arguing the space will look more like the fabric of computing and apps than a single collapse into one or two apps like ChatGPT or Claude.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: A directional disagreement with the dominant Silicon Valley monopoly playbook; underpins a diversified multi-layer venture strategy.
insights.quote: if you talk like Peter Teal he'd be like competitions for losers you know the I want to find a monopoly

insights.claim: Grossman expects that within five years there will be multiple massive public AI companies in health, legal, finance and industrials that are not the big labs, plus a large category of 'middleware' companies handling memory, harnesses, orchestration and payments in the agentic stack.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Concrete five-year map of where new public-market winners could emerge outside the frontier labs; middleware is called out as 'just getting going'.
insights.quote: Five years from now, I think we will see a number of massive public companies in the um in the in the consumer AI or the business AI space, whether that's health, legal, finance that aren't the big labs

insights.claim: USV also invests at the energy layer as a cross-cutting input to all AI, and has been doing so for six or seven years, viewing the big labs and their models as the infrastructure of the 'next internet' to be built around.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Signals energy as a durable AI-adjacent investment vector; USV positions itself around rather than against the labs.
insights.quote: we're also investing at the energy layer um because energy is an input you know across the board for for all AI

insights.claim: Grossman calls the competitive threat from frontier labs the 'AI kill zone' and argues companies closest to the models' core skills — language, media, law, code, natively digital domains — are the most exposed, while specialization and real-world connectivity build defensibility even inside those areas.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: A map of which AI startups are most vulnerable to lab encroachment versus which can defend via domain depth; a screening lens for early-stage risk.
insights.quote: we refer to it as the kill zone, the AI kill zone

insights.claim: Grossman's bet is that frontier labs, however capable, cannot be best at every use case, so domain expertise, defensibility and connectivity to the real world let specialized companies pull products in the right direction against general-purpose lab offerings.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Core rationale for backing vertical AI apps over horizontal lab products; expertise at the edges is the moat.
insights.quote: the idea of being the best at every use case is is too much too much to chew

insights.claim: Pompliano observes that when OpenAI launched a product competing with his AI CFO Sylvia, it lacked core investor-facing functionality despite being exactly what an engineer would build from available connectivity tools like Plaid and SnapTrade, illustrating that vertical AI success depends on solving customer-pain problems, not just engineering ones.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano, host and builder of Sylvia
insights.so_what: Real operator evidence that lab entrants can miss domain-specific needs, supporting the specialization defensibility thesis.

insights.claim: Building a robot that patrols a factory requires general reasoning, spatial reasoning and local mobility across cloud and on-device models, leading Grossman to conclude the world is going to be 'multimodel' with most use cases combining general-purpose and specialized models.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Undercuts a single-model-wins narrative and supports orchestration/routing tooling as an investable layer.
insights.quote: the world is going to be multimodel and most use cases are going to be multimodel a combination of general purpose and specialized

insights.claim: Pompliano says his company was one of the fastest-growing Claude customers by percentage and was spending more on compute than a large public company, prompting an internal task force to drive token costs down as CEOs increasingly flag compute cost as too expensive.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano, host
insights.so_what: Signals that AI inference spend is becoming a board-level cost line, driving demand for cost-optimization and routing solutions.
insights.quote: you're spending more than us like a really big public company and I was like oh that's a problem

insights.claim: Grossman says intelligent model routing optimizes both cost and quality, citing a report where an open-source coding harness running on a mix of models outperformed Claude Code and Opus, and predicting far more sophisticated agent-and-model orchestration as workloads move from prototype to production.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Reframes routing as a performance lever, not just cost savings; orchestration/harness startups become more valuable as production deployment scales.
insights.quote: when you can intelligently use a mix of models. You can not only optimize your cost, you can improve your performance uh by using the best model for the best task

insights.claim: USV built its own internal VC platform over nine months in which every deal, company, idea and person has an attached agent, orchestrated in software so thousands of agents operate under the hood rather than a single chatbot.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: A concrete production example of the multi-agent future; demonstrates internal tooling built cheaply and fast, a template for enterprise agent adoption.
insights.quote: every uh deal, company, idea, uh potentially person in our platform has an agent attached to it and they're all orchestrated through software

insights.claim: In USV's system a main 'deal analyst' agent lives in email and messaging like a teammate, while sub-agents dedicated to each deal run overnight — listening to Twitter, web searching, reading notes — and push their work into a shared context memory layer for other agents to use.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Illustrates the emerging architecture (orchestrator + specialized sub-agents + shared memory) that middleware startups will need to serve.
insights.quote: that agent wakes up and goes out and and listens to Twitter and listen and does web searching

insights.claim: Grossman argues AI is shifting from a chat-based call-and-response paradigm to a trigger-based proactive paradigm, where agents fire off timers or events (a meeting note, an inbound email) and can execute longer-running, more autonomous tasks as they grow more capable.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: A directional shift in product design; watch for tooling that supports event-driven, long-running agent workflows.
insights.quote: we're moving from um a kind of a chatbased call response paradigm to more of a proact to like a trigger-based paradigm

insights.claim: Grossman argues venture investing is too human and episodic to fully automate, but follow-on dollars that fill the edges of rounds — the last few hundred thousand or million — could be deployed by a 'pool of capital agent' following parameters and acting autonomously, and a USV portfolio company is pursuing this.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Points to a near-term wedge for automated capital in venture — parameter-driven follow-on allocation triggered by tier-one lead signals.
insights.quote: one area where I could totally see more automated uh capital coming into venture deals is like filling in the edges of venture rounds

insights.claim: Grossman says the first fleet of investing agents traded crypto because those markets are programmatic and accessible, and that agents with wallets making payments as autonomous financial actors will proliferate fastest in the most open, API- or crypto-rail-accessible markets.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Frames crypto and prediction markets as the natural first venue for autonomous agent trading — a watch area for agent-native financial infrastructure.
insights.quote: the places where that's going to be the easiest are the markets that are the most open and accessible either via API or because they're, you know, on crypto rails

insights.claim: Both Grossman and Pompliano report that naive prompting to pick a winning stock has produced few success stories, while the working cases combine a human's original idea or industry view with AI to find and express it — mirroring how great investors process complex data into a thesis.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano and Nick Grossman
insights.so_what: Tempers hype around fully autonomous stock-picking; the durable edge remains human-idea-plus-AI-execution, not pure model prompting.
insights.quote: really what he's using is again it's kind of his idea that he's then using to find the idea

insights.claim: Grossman knows a team using agents to trade frontier and prediction markets and obscure corners where retail dominates, betting agents can spot patterns before big institutions arrive and arb them out, and says those traders are making some money at early scale.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Suggests dislocated, retail-heavy markets are where agentic alpha currently exists, before institutional arbitrage compresses it.
insights.quote: corners of prediction markets and other kind of obscure places where maybe there's some early dislocations

insights.claim: Pompliano relays Jordi Visser's idea that because AI leaders like Jensen Huang give roughly five interviews a week, feeding those transcripts into models to predict beneficiary companies is 'scary accurate,' citing that a Gavin Baker mention of trainium as an H2 trend led a model to pick Marvell, which rose about 100% in 60 days.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano, host, relaying Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A replicable AI research workflow — mine executive interview transcripts for downstream beneficiaries — with a concrete example of a large move.
insights.quote: If you take those and you take the transcripts and you put them into the systems and you say ... what are the companies that are going to benefit over the next 12 months

insights.claim: Grossman suggests a lab could develop a frontier model and not sell it as an API or consumer app but internalize it as a trading fund, noting SSI is rumored to have done something like this, and that internalizing superintelligence could prove a better business model than the ones familiar today.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: A non-obvious risk/opportunity: the best models may never reach the market if labs monetize them internally, changing how AI value is captured.
insights.quote: you could in theory develop a frontier model and not sell it and just turn it into a trading fund

insights.claim: Grossman notes vertical integration is running in both directions: application-layer companies with unique data are moving down into training models — citing Revolut training a foundation model and the Robinhood chain — while big labs move up into the application layer.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Signals that scaled app companies with proprietary data are a new class of model builders; the model layer is not the labs' exclusive territory.
insights.quote: revolute getting into the model game was not something I think a lot of people were thinking about before they announced that that's like the opposite direction going down from the application layer

insights.claim: Grossman says early-stage founders using enterprise plans with the best data-retention policies and parallelizing across models focus mostly on capability and user experience, and that structural data-protection worries surface mainly at scale, which is why the concern is loudest from the biggest companies.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Reframes the data-privacy debate as a scale-stage concern; app-layer companies uniquely see a data stack that never reaches the model.
insights.quote: there's a whole stack of data that never reaches the actual model itself right which application layer companies can can uniquely see and learn from

insights.claim: Grossman argues selling data to the labs works for some categories — UGC companies like Reddit and Stack Overflow, and data-generation firms like Scale AI — but is prone to commoditization, whereas a USV portfolio company using a patented DNA-synthesis chemical process to generate biomodel training data is a more defensible, specialized example.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: A defensibility test for data businesses: patented, specialized data generation beats commodity labeling; USV is broadly less excited about selling data to labs.
insights.quote: that's an example to me that feels more defensible and like specialized um versus just sort of doing work out in the world

insights.claim: Grossman treats a model's philosophy or personality — beyond performance, cost and data security — as a fourth vector of competition, noting Western models embody capitalism and risk-taking while Eastern models may lean more socialist, so identical capabilities can still yield different weights and answers.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman and Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: An underexplored differentiation axis for model selection, especially in high-stakes domains like finance, therapy and healthcare.

insights.claim: USV portfolio company Cradle runs nuanced model challenges to tease out model personalities and published a report on which models lie the most, finding one model lied most in its first test — the kind of trait not captured by standard software-engineering benchmarks focused on coding ability.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: As agents take on non-coding tasks, behavioral/trust benchmarks become a new evaluation category and potential investable niche.
insights.quote: which models lie to you the most

insights.claim: Grossman expects a clearer picture of the model layer within five years and questions whether it will be just Anthropic plus Chinese open-source models or whether US open models can emerge, noting USV has invested in a project attempting decentralized, open model training.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Flags US open-source and decentralized training as a strategic gap and a USV bet against a two-pole (closed-US / open-China) model landscape.
insights.quote: is it open anthropic plus some open source Chinese models and that's it or can we develop open models here in the US

insights.claim: Grossman says he is equal parts excited and scared about AI, arguing that like the steam engine, automobile and internet, AI will unleash waves of unforeseen second-order implications — data centers as the biggest new energy consumers straining the grid, and large-scale job displacement raising unanswered questions about how humanity shares the gains.
insights.speaker: Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Ventures
insights.so_what: Grounds the buildout's macro risks — grid strain and labor disruption — as investable and political flashpoints, with an ownership-model question left open.
insights.quote: I am honestly like equal parts excited and scared

insights.claim: Pompliano argues the underexplored AI opportunity is augmenting the patient or consumer rather than only the professional — recounting that AI correctly caught his wife's doctor being wrong — creating a consumer-protection angle distinct from the job-displacement narrative.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano, host
insights.so_what: Points to consumer-side 'personal superpowers' products (e.g. verify-your-doctor, car-buying advisors) as a distinct AI market beyond replacing professionals.
insights.quote: there's like a displacement argument, but there's also like a consumer protection argument

insights.claim: Both note Neuralink has reportedly performed 26 brain implants, and frame future neural implants as 'the new steroids' that could give mental-performance advantages and eventually require regulation in competitive settings.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano and Nick Grossman
insights.so_what: A speculative but concrete data point on brain-computer-interface adoption and a framing (enhanced-games/regulation) for how augmentation markets could develop.
insights.quote: Neuralink supposedly has done 26 of them
